# Medical Education

Research and news on artificial intelligence in medical education.

Items are selected nightly by an automated pipeline and link to their original sources. See [About](../about.md) for how selection works.

<img class="section-banner" src="../../assets/section-medical-education.svg" alt="">

<div class="section-brief">
<p>Medical students increasingly recognize the need for a standardized artificial intelligence curriculum to bridge the gap between enthusiasm about AI and actual competence, as evidenced by a systematic review and meta-analysis <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42310669?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260618141138&amp;v=2.20.0">[3]</a>. Large language models have demonstrated substantial agreement with human supervisors in providing feedback on clinical clerkship logs, indicating their reliability in assessment contexts <a href="https://mededu.jmir.org/2026/1/e90064">[5]</a>. In formative evaluation, there is comparable performance and similar student perceptions of multiple-choice questions generated by both large language models and human students <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42304363?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260617134036&amp;v=2.20.0">[4]</a>. Additionally, generative AI has been shown to enhance medical higher education learning performance via mechanisms consistent with social cognitive theory <a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42292247?fc=20260609215449&amp;ff=20260615160740&amp;v=2.20.0">[9]</a>.</p>
<p class="section-brief-date">The picture as of June 18, 2026; numbered links go to the items below.</p>
</div>

    <p class="news-card-summary">Large language models show substantial potential in infectious disease education and decision support, with ability to handle both exam and clinical scenarios.</p>

    <p class="news-card-summary">Systematic review finds medical students recognize need for standardized artificial intelligence curriculum to address gap between enthusiasm and actual competence.</p>
